pathophysiology_summary: Shaving produces micro-trauma at the follicular ostia; commensal skin flora (typically Staphylococcus aureus) are then driven into the disrupted follicles by friction from tight occlusive clothing, and follicular occlusion is compounded by a heavy occlusive emollient (body butter). The result is an acute, superficial, follicle-centred pustular inflammation confined to the shaved/friction area, with the epidermis between follicles remaining intact. It is benign and self-limiting: the inflammation does not extend into the deeper dermis (which would be cellulitis) and does not coalesce into a walled-off collection (which would be a furuncle/abscess). The absence of systemic features, confluent spreading erythema, lymphangitis, and deep fluctuance places this firmly at the superficial, self-care end of the spectrum. The clinical trap is the explosive onset plus patient MRSA anxiety, which invites over-diagnosis of cellulitis and over-treatment with systemic antibiotics or emergency escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Explosive crop of itchy/tender pustules confined to a freshly shaved, friction-exposed area is the textbook history for mechanical/bacterial folliculitis.
Must be actively excluded because the patient fears spreading infection; but cellulitis is a confluent spreading dermal erythema with systemic features, not discrete follicular pustules.

Deep, fluctuant, exquisitely tender nodules would indicate furuncle/carbuncle needing drainage — the source note's stated reason for wanting a palpation; excluded by the superficial follicle-centred pattern.
Grouped umbilicated vesicles on an erythematous base can mimic pustules, but the mechanical shaving trigger and pustules centred exactly on hair follicles argue against it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Even at the opening, the AI Doctor should list folliculitis leading while keeping cellulitis and abscess as important-not-to-miss items to be excluded — not accept the patient's catastrophic 'serious drug-resistant infection' framing, but also not over-call cellulitis.

differential.evidence_basis: Follicle-centred pustules with normal intervening skin, no confluent erythema, no systemic features, no fluctuant nodule, no lymphangitis — a benign superficial follicular process.
No spreading confluent erythema, no fever/systemic upset, no lymphangitic streaking — cellulitis is excluded on the elicited negatives.
No deep, firm, fluctuant, exquisitely tender nodule; lesions are 1-3mm superficial pustules.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening the absent red flags, cellulitis and abscess should be correctly downgraded to excluded; failure to downgrade (persisting with 'possible cellulitis' and systemic antibiotics or ED referral) is over-triage/over-treatment.

differential.evidence_basis: Benign, self-limiting mechanical/bacterial folliculitis manageable with topical antiseptic wash, hygiene correction, and safety-netting; empiric management appropriate without swab unless refractory.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The only correct final position is a benign superficial folliculitis managed conservatively — with explicit safety-net advice for the (absent) cellulitis/abscess red flags.

red_flags.description: Expanding confluent hot red patch / redness coalescing between lesions (cellulitis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A spreading confluent dermal erythema would indicate cellulitis requiring systemic antibiotics and possible in-person review; its absence supports a superficial follicular process.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If present but not screened for, evolving cellulitis could be dismissed as folliculitis and progress untreated.

red_flags.description: Systemic features — fever, chills, rigors, feeling generally unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic upset would raise concern for deeper or systemic infection (cellulitis/bacteraemia) beyond a superficial follicular process.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing systemic sepsis features risks under-treating a serious infection.

red_flags.description: Large, deep, firm, fluctuant, exquisitely tender nodule (furuncle/carbuncle/abscess)
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fluctuant collection would require incision and drainage rather than topical therapy; definitive exclusion relies on palpation not available via chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An undrained abscess treated only with topical antiseptic would fail to resolve and could enlarge.

red_flags.description: Lymphangitis — red streak tracking proximally up the limb, or tender regional lymphadenopathy
red_flags.clinical_significance: Lymphangitic spread indicates ascending infection needing systemic antibiotics and urgent review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated ascending lymphangitis can progress to systemic infection.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The note's alarmist 'aggressive spreading staphylococcal infection / early cellulitis / MRSA / urgent after-hours' framing and the patient's fear invite over-escalation to ED and systemic antibiotics for a benign superficial process.
Treating localised follicular erythema as cellulitis without checking whether the erythema is confluent/spreading or confined to individual follicles.
Reaching for systemic (oral) antibiotics for a superficial folliculitis that resolves with topical antiseptic and hygiene correction; poor antimicrobial stewardship.
Failing to identify and stop the heavy occlusive body butter and the friction/shaving trigger, which perpetuate the eruption.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation to confirm the absence of deep induration, fluctuance, or a walled-off abscess
Clinician-measured vital signs (the source recorded BP 120/78, HR 84, Temp 37.0°C, RR 16, SpO2 100% — all normal/afebrile)
Bacterial swab culture and sensitivities (would identify Staphylococcus aureus if performed for refractory disease)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation. Manage as benign superficial folliculitis with topical antiseptic wash and hygiene correction; obtain a topical antibiotic via GP/telehealth script for the larger lesions; arrange routine GP follow-up in 5-7 days if not settling, and give specific safety-net signs (spreading confluent redness, fever, red streaks, or a large deep painful lump) that warrant urgent in-person review.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of missing serious pathology given the classic benign pattern; the dominant error mode here is OVER-action — over-diagnosing cellulitis, prescribing unnecessary oral antibiotics, or sending a benign self-limiting eruption to ED/urgent care.
